<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Elaheh,
You didn't need to wait a week, just do
squeue -u em667
JOBID PARTITION     NAME     USER ST       TIME  NODES NODELIST(REASON)
8376336   skylake s2.8t5c5    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376337   skylake s2.8t6c5    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376338   skylake s2.8t7c5    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376339   skylake s2.8t8c5    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376340   skylake s2.8t9c5    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376341   skylake s2.8t10c    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376342   skylake s2.8t11c    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8376343   skylake s2.8t12c    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8455552   skylake s3.3c5d4    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
8455553   skylake s3.3c5d4    em667 PD       0:00      8 (AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit)
The AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit pending reason indicates that you have insufficient  hours remaining, which indeed you do - each of these jobs is requesting 36 hours and 32*8 cpus, i.e. 9216 cpu hours, whereas your balance in 
FERRARI-SL2-CPU is less than 6000. It doesn't matter that your jobs may not actually run for 36 hours, all SLURM can do is take the job spec at face value, which is one reason why job timelimits should be made realistic.
The choices here are to either reduce the size*duration of the jobs, or to buy more cpu hours for FERRARI-SL2-CPU.
The next quarter begins on 1st Feb which is when FERRARI-SL3-CPU will be replenished.
Best regards
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

[SUMMARY] Jobs hanging in queue

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support,
It seems that I reached some kind of limit, my jobs are hanging in the queue with reason “AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit”.
It is true that we are a bit short of remaining CPU hours (on holmes-sl2-cpu account), but according to mybalance we have more than 8k hours left. None of my jobs are exceeding this, there is one big job (13735893) that is scheduled for 36 hours with 32 CPU, but even that is way much less than 8k. 
Should I put all my jobs to SL3 or is there a way to use up the remaining 8k hours?
Thanks in advance for the help,
Regards,
Lajos
---Lajos Kalmar PhD, Senior Research AssociateBacterial Infection Group, Department of Veterinary Medicine, University of Cambridge
Madingley Road, Cambridge, CB3 0ES, United Kingdom
Mobile: +44 7864 326261, [E-mail: lk397@cam.ac.uk](mailto:lk397@cam.ac.uk)

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_PRINCIPAL_INVESTIGATOR>
@Stuart Rankin - Not done any digging but sounds suspiciously like the mismatch between the account databases?
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Lajos,
There is a known issue with SLURM in which the controller can get ahead of the database when calculating historical usage which means that it stops scheduling jobs prematurely. I have corrected this for your project and several pending jobs have now started.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Procurement / GPU Resource Purchase / Quotation
Technical Action Required: No (user to follow published process)
Finance Action Required: Yes (user departmental PO required)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests:
- A quote for 200 SL2-GPU hours
- Creation of an SL2-GPU account under a named PI

GPU hours and account creation follow the published charges and procurement process. Pricing and ordering instructions are provided on the official charges page.

ACTION

1) Direct user to official pricing page:
   https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/charges

2) Advise user to:
   - Review the published SL2-GPU pricing.
   - Follow the process for raising a purchase order via their departmental finance team.
   - Submit required account creation details as described on the charges page.

3) Do NOT:
   - Generate ad hoc pricing outside published rates.
   - Create accounts before procurement process is followed.
   - Escalate unless pricing discrepancy or policy exception is requested.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Fiona,

Please follow the instructions and pricing information available on our charges page:

https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/charges

This page outlines the process for purchasing SL2-GPU hours and for requesting account creation. Once your departmental finance team has raised the appropriate purchase order, we can proceed accordingly.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not provide unofficial pricing.
- Do not create accounts without the required procurement steps.
- Ensure users follow the documented purchase workflow.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Resource Purchase / Account Creation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The process for purchasing SL2 GPU hours and creating a new account involves raising a purchase order (PO) and sending it to the HPC support team. The PO should include the name of the project, the PI, and the quantity of GPU hours. An informal email quote can be provided if needed [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the details with the user:
   - Quantity: 200 SL2 GPU hours
   - PI name: Dr. Tian Zhao (crsid: txz20)
   - Lab/group: Zhao Lab
   - Institute: (Ask for the institute name)
2. Provide an informal email quote if requested.
3. Instruct the user to raise a PO and send it to purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
4. Once the PO is received, create the new SL2 GPU account under Dr. Tian Zhao’s name (ZHAO-SL2-GPU).
5. Credit the account with the purchased GPU hours.
6. Send the PO to the finance department for processing.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the name of the institute?
- Do you need an informal email quote for the purchase?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Fiona,

To proceed with the purchase of 200 SL2 GPU hours and the creation of an SL2 GPU account under Dr. Tian Zhao’s name (crsid: txz20), please provide the following details:
- Quantity: 200 SL2 GPU hours
- PI name: Dr. Tian Zhao (crsid: txz20)
- Lab/group: Zhao Lab
- Institute: (Please provide the name of the institute)

We can provide an informal email quote if needed. Once you have the details, please raise a purchase order (PO) and send it to purchases@hpc.cam.ac.uk. We will then create the new SL2 GPU account (ZHAO-SL2-GPU) and credit it with the purchased GPU hours.

Please let us know if you have any further questions.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure all required details are included in the PO to avoid delays in processing [1][2][3][4][5].
- Follow the documented process for creating new SL2 GPU accounts and crediting hours [1][2][3][4][5].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-83224
[2] : HPCSSUP-53378
[3] : HPCSSUP-98046
[4] : HPCSSUP-64561
[5] : HPCSSUP-56835

benchmark_annotation.summary: SSH login requests TOTP but MFA not set up yet

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / Account Provisioning / MFA
Technical Action Required: No (account application required)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (user cannot complete login)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports:
- Able to reach login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
- Prompted for TOTP
- Has not received QR code
- login-web reports invalid username/password
- multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk disconnects after password

This strongly indicates that the user does not yet have a fully provisioned CSD3 account with MFA enrolment enabled.

MFA (TOTP) enrolment occurs only after an account has been formally created via the official HPC application process.

ACTION

1) Confirm whether the user has applied for an HPC account
   Direct them to:
   https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application

2) Explain that:
   - Account creation must be triggered via the official application form.
   - MFA enrolment (QR code) is generated only after the account is provisioned.
   - The behaviour they are seeing is consistent with an account not yet fully created.

3) Do NOT:
   - Reset MFA (no MFA exists yet).
   - Attempt manual account creation via ticket.
   - Troubleshoot login-web further until account status is confirmed.

4) If the user confirms they have already applied:
   - Check provisioning status internally.
   - Escalate only if application is stuck in provisioning.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: SSH login requests TOTP but MFA not set up yet

Hello Jin,

Have you previously applied for an account via the official HPC application form?

https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application

If not, please complete this form with your details and your PI’s details and submit it. This will trigger the account provisioning process. Once your account is created, you will then be able to enrol in MFA (you will be presented with a QR code during login).

If you have already submitted the form, please let us know and we can check the status for you.

Best regards,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume MFA malfunction when account may not yet exist.
- Do not attempt to bypass the official account creation workflow.
- MFA enrolment is tied to successful account provisioning.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with CSD3 MFA setup, including not receiving a QR code, login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk reporting invalid username or password, and connection issues with multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk. Similar issues have been resolved by resetting the MFA configuration and guiding the user through the setup process [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's account status to ensure it is not locked [5].
2. Reset the user's MFA configuration [1][2][3].
3. Schedule an ID check through a Teams call to verify the user's identity before resetting the MFA configuration [1].
4. Provide the user with instructions to set up MFA by logging into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and following the documentation [1][2][3][4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried logging in using your Raven password? [4]
- Can you confirm if you have any existing TOTP entries in your authenticator app? [2][3]
- Please provide a screenshot of the error message when attempting to log in to login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk [2].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Jin,

Thank you for your email. We have reviewed your issue and it appears that we need to reset your MFA configuration to resolve the login problems. Before proceeding, we need to verify your identity through a Teams call. Please let us know if you are available for a quick call today between 2:30-4pm.

Once your identity is verified, we will reset your MFA configuration and provide you with instructions to set it up again. Please follow the steps outlined in our documentation: [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA) — CSD3 1.0 documentation](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk).

Please get back in touch if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the user's identity before resetting MFA configurations [1].
- Ensure the user follows the correct documentation for MFA setup [1][2][3][4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-100437
[2] : HPCSSUP-97014
[3] : HPCSSUP-50032
[4] : HPCSSUP-73535
[5] : HPCSSUP-52145

Could you please reset my MFA/TOTP configuration so I can initialise it again?

Lina Robinet

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Unable to initialise CSD3 TOTP MFA via multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear CSD3 Support Team,
I am an internal Cambridge user and am trying to log in to CSD3 for the first time.
According to the onboarding instructions, I should first SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk to generate a TOTP MFA secret. However, when I attempt to SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk using my CRSid and UIS password, the connection is closed immediately after authentication.
As a result, I am unable to generate a TOTP secret or complete MFA setup, and I am also unable to log in to the CSD3 login nodes.
Could you please reset or initialise my CSD3 MFA/TOTP status so that I can complete the first-time MFA setup via multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk?
My details:
*
CRSid: mm2945
*
Institution:  University of Cambridge IMS TVP lab
Many thanks for your help.
Best regards,
Millie

[TICKET_SUMMARY] MFA not working on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support,  
I am trying to use MFA to log in to CSD3. I went through your setup instructions some time ago, and had the message that I already have two-factor authentication set up on my account. However today when I go to login, the one-time password codes are apparently incorrect and I cannot login.   
For additional context, in my microsoft authenticator app I only have 'University of Cambridge rw660@cam.ac.uk' and no user named 'CSD3...'. I however get no QR code to set this up.  
Best wishes, 
Rebecca

[TICKET_SUMMARY] MFA for CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support Team
I’m currently trying to login for the first time to CSD3, but while doing SSH login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk<[http://multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)> during TOTP configuration, the message doesn’t give the QR code or the secret key for MFA set up. Instead it displays a Warning message (as displayed in the online Walkthrough) followed by a blank line of “Password: ". Could you please help me with setting this up? Thanks.
Best regards,
Kevin

ssh ljr64@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```
and you will be presented with a QR code to show your authenticator application. NB: the SSH TOTP is distinct from the one used for login-web: do not delete your existing TOTP (if you have one for login-web). For further guidance, please follow  https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html. 
Please also note: we are aware of an issue where the QR code does not render correctly in some terminal windows. If this happens, please do not exit the session: you can manually enter the secret key given beneath the QR code.
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support

benchmark_annotation.summary: Running TensorFlow on ampere: "module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6" not found.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / TensorFlow / Ampere GPU / Modules
Technical Action Required: No (user environment clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is attempting to follow the official TensorFlow GPU instructions:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/tensorflow.html

They report that:

  module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

“doesn’t work”.

The documentation indicates that these steps must be run within an interactive session on an Ampere GPU node. The Python module is available within the Ampere software stack. If the module cannot be loaded, this is most likely due to the environment context (e.g. not being on an Ampere node or the module environment having been altered).

There is no evidence that the documentation is outdated or that the module has been removed.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm execution context

Ask the user to confirm they are running the instructions inside an interactive session on an Ampere GPU node.

Example:

  srun --partition=ampere --gres=gpu:1 --time=01:00:00 --pty bash

Then check module availability:

  module avail python/3.8.11

Expected output should include:

  python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

2) Attempt module load

If the module appears in module avail, attempt:

  module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

3) If the module still does not load

Ask the user to provide the exact error message produced when running:

  module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

and confirm:
  - The hostname (to verify they are on an Ampere GPU node)
  - Whether they have modified the module environment (e.g. using module purge)

Only escalate if:
  - The module does not appear in module avail while on an Ampere node, or
  - The load error indicates a system-side module configuration issue.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello William,

The TensorFlow instructions must be run within an interactive session on an Ampere GPU node.

Could you please start an Ampere session, for example:

  srun --partition=ampere --gres=gpu:1 --time=01:00:00 --pty bash

and then run:

  module avail python/3.8.11

You should see:

  python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

If it appears but does not load, please send us the exact error message and the output of:

  hostname

so we can investigate further.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume the module has been removed without checking module avail.
- Do not recommend installing a custom Python or modifying system module paths.
- Keep troubleshooting limited to confirming correct execution context and module visibility.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / TensorFlow Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is attempting to run TensorFlow on Ampere GPUs but is encountering issues with the module load command. The documentation provided may be outdated or incomplete for the current setup [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the current available modules for TensorFlow and CUDA compatibility.
2. Provide updated instructions for loading the correct modules.
3. If necessary, escalate to a technical specialist for further assistance.

ACTION
Direct the user to use the following commands to load the necessary modules and create a virtual environment:
```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-gpu
module load cuda/12.1 cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1
virtualenv tensorflow-env
source tensorflow-env/bin/activate
pip install tensorflow
```

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello William,

Thank you for your message. The documentation you are following may be outdated for the current setup. Please try the following commands to load the necessary modules and create a virtual environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-gpu
module load cuda/12.1 cudnn/8.9_cuda-12.1
virtualenv tensorflow-env
source tensorflow-env/bin/activate
pip install tensorflow
```

Please let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is provided with the most up-to-date instructions for running TensorFlow on Ampere GPUs.
- If the issue persists, consider escalating to a technical specialist for further investigation.

[1] : HPCSSUP-56286
[2] : HPCSSUP-78640

retrieval_trace.text:
[TICKET_SUMMARY] Tensorflow Help

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear IT Support,
I am Theo Lewy (CRSID tal43) and my PI is Rich Kerswell (project KERSWELL-SL3).
I am currently trying to get tensorflow working on the ampere GPUs but I am having trouble with this. The documentation only talks about rhel7, and I am getting conflicts when I follow the commands in “Running Tensorflow with GPU acceleration on CSD3” and pip install dependencies ([https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/tensorflow.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/tensorflow.html)) so I have instead been trying to set it up using rhel8.
I have currently tried using the attached .sh script. The attached slurm script is my output. The error is (I think) due to the fact that all of the modules I have loaded use cuda-11.4, while tensorflow-2.11.0 is built to be compatible with cuda-11.2.
I cannot unload cuda-11.4 without getting the message “ERROR: cuda/11.4 cannot be unloaded due to a prereq", and so I can’t work out what set of modules should by loaded so that I can run tensorflow on the ampere GPU nodes. <MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Theo,
You can use the command “module avail 2>&1 | grep cuda” to find the available cuda versions installed in the cluster. If you want to load a different module to cuda/11.4.0 that is included with rhel8/default-amp then you can simply load the new module you need and it will take precedence over the cuda version in the default ampere module. Make sure to load the correct cudnn version for the tensorflow version you want to use. 
As you mention the instructions on our documentation are for the older rhel7 pascal GPU nodes. You can use different versions of both cuda and cudnn for running tensorflow in the Ampere GPU nodes. You can also have a look at the tensorflow documentation and install the latest cuda and cudnn in your own python environment before installing the latest tensorflow version.
Kind regards,
Avraam

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Login / Account Lockout
Technical Action Required: No (diagnosis + user guidance)
Escalation Required: No (unless lock persists beyond normal timeout)
Urgency: Medium (user access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports:
- Previously able to log into CSD3 headnodes.
- After an idle period, login fails.
- After entering password and MFA, system prompts for password again.
- Appears as though password is incorrect.

This pattern is consistent with an account lock due to multiple unsuccessful login attempts. On CSD3, repeated failed authentication attempts can trigger a temporary lock, which then clears automatically after a timeout period.

A possible contributing factor is that the user may be entering an outdated UIS/Raven password; if they recently changed it, the new one should be used on CSD3 as well.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Check account status (internal)
   - Confirm whether the account is in a locked state due to failed attempts.
   - Confirm there is no wider authentication incident.

2) Inform user of temporary lock behaviour
   - Explain that repeated failed login attempts can trigger a temporary lock.
   - State that the lock clears automatically after approximately 30 minutes.
   - No manual reset is required unless the lock persists.

3) Clarify correct credential usage
   - User must enter their current UIS/Raven password (not an old HPC-specific password).
   - After password, they must provide the correct TOTP associated with CSD3 (e.g. SSH token).

4) If issue persists after timeout
   Request:
     - Exact hostname used (e.g. login.hpc.cam.ac.uk, login-icelake, multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk)
     - Exact error message shown after failure
     - Confirmation that UIS/Raven password works for other services

5) Escalate only if:
   - Lock does not clear after timeout window
   - Account shows unexpected authentication failure state
   - MFA system appears inconsistent

DO NOT:
- Reset the password.
- Reset MFA without evidence.
- Suggest creating a new account.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: CSD3 login issue

Dear Vijay,

Your symptoms are consistent with a temporary account lock caused by multiple unsuccessful login attempts. If that is the case, the lock should clear automatically after approximately 30 minutes.

Once it has cleared, please ensure that you are entering your current UIS/Raven password when prompted in the terminal, followed by your CSD3 MFA code.

If you continue to experience issues after waiting for the lock to clear, please let us know the exact hostname you are connecting to and the full error message shown.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not disclose internal authentication logs.
- Do not confirm password correctness.
- Avoid manual intervention unless lock persists abnormally.
- UIS/Raven password changes automatically propagate to CSD3 authentication.

1. Check the system logs for any errors related to the user's login attempts.
2. Verify the user's MFA configuration to ensure they are using the correct TOTP code for SSH login.
3. If necessary, reset the user's MFA and guide them through the process of setting up a new TOTP code for SSH login [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are you using the correct TOTP code for SSH login (labeled "CSD3: SSH Login")?
- Have you tried logging in from a different terminal or network?
- Can you provide the output of the `ssh -vvv` command to help diagnose the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Vijay,

Thank you for reporting this issue. It seems like you might be experiencing an issue with your MFA configuration or session. Could you please confirm if you are using the correct TOTP code for SSH login (labeled "CSD3: SSH Login")? Additionally, could you try logging in from a different terminal or network to see if the issue persists?

If the problem continues, please provide the output of the `ssh -vvv` command to help us diagnose the issue further.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not reset the user's MFA without verifying their identity and ensuring they understand the process of setting up a new TOTP code [5].
- Ensure that the user is using the correct TOTP code for SSH login, as using the wrong code can cause login issues [1][2][3][4][5].

----Original Message----
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: Wednesday, April 17, 2024 11:23 PM
To: Caijin Chen <cc2123@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (cc2123)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7 Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy addition

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / New Platform Provisioning / Resource Request
Technical Action Required: Yes (new SRCP platform setup)
Portal/Form Action Required: Yes (SRCP resource request form submission)
Escalation Required: Yes (SRCP platform provisioning team)
Urgency: Medium–High (students starting next week; high IP / Level 3 data)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The PI is requesting the creation of a new SRCP Level 3 platform (high IP value) for a new project (TumourVue), separate from an existing platform (Predict), with funding in place for one year. Three Masters students will require access with permissions similar to an existing workflow (Python-based image analysis).

This is not a simple user-access request. It requires submission of an SRCP resource request form to initiate provisioning of a new platform, including:
- Platform creation
- Security level confirmation (Level 3)
- Funding confirmation
- Storage and compute allocation
- Role definitions

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Clarify request type
   - Confirm that this is a new SRCP platform (not just additional users).
   - Confirm that Level 3 classification is required.

2) Direct PI to SRCP resource request form
   Provide link:
     https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/form/srcp-resource-request

   Explain that this form initiates:
     - New platform provisioning
     - Security review
     - Resource allocation
     - Funding linkage

3) Provide documentation for security levels
   Refer to:
     https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/user-onboarding/index.html#user-onboarding

   Clarify:
     - Level 3 environments are designed for high IP / sensitive research data.
     - Appropriate role selection must be specified for student users.

4) After submission (internal)
   - Confirm form submission received.
   - Attach updates to the resource request ticket.
   - Escalate to SRCP provisioning team.
   - Do not promise specific timelines until confirmed.

5) Do NOT:
   - Manually create the platform outside the formal workflow.
   - Add users before platform provisioning is complete.
   - Confirm approval until security and provisioning checks are complete.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NOT CLEAR IN FORM)
- Confirm expected duration (1 year initially).
- Confirm estimated storage requirements.
- Confirm number of users and roles required.
- Confirm whether this is fully isolated from existing Predict platform.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: TumourVue SRCP Platform Request

Dear Gita,

To create a new SRCP platform for TumourVue, you will need to submit an SRCP resource request using the following form:

  https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/form/srcp-resource-request

This will initiate the provisioning process for a new Level 3 platform, including security review and resource allocation.

You may also find the following documentation helpful when selecting the appropriate level and roles:

  https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/user-onboarding/index.html#user-onboarding

Once the form has been submitted, we will process the request and provide updates via the associated ticket.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the SRCP resource request process.
- Ensure Level 3 designation is formally captured in the request.
- Do not grant access prior to completion of provisioning and security approval.
- Avoid committing to deadlines until the provisioning team confirms scheduling.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The request involves setting up a level 3 SRCP platform for TumourVue - cancer surgery trials data, with access for three master's students. The data sharing agreement is in place, and the request is high priority due to the imminent start of the students' work [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the request to the appropriate team for SRCP platform setup.
2. Confirm the exact requirements and timeline for the setup with the user.
3. Ensure that the data sharing agreement and any necessary approvals are in place.
4. Provide the user with a timeline for the setup and any next steps they need to take.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Gita,

Thank you for your request. We have escalated your request for setting up a level 3 SRCP platform for TumourVue - cancer surgery trials data. We will confirm the exact requirements and timeline for the setup with you shortly.

Please ensure that the data sharing agreement and any necessary approvals are in place. We will provide you with a timeline for the setup and any next steps you need to take.

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Ensure that all necessary approvals and data sharing agreements are in place before proceeding with the setup [1].
- Follow the established procedures for setting up SRCP platforms to ensure compliance with data protection regulations [1][2][3][4][5].

This will initiate the provisioning process for a new Level 3 platform, including security review and resource allocation.

You may also find the following documentation helpful when selecting the appropriate level and roles:

  https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/user-onboarding/index.html#user-onboarding

Once the form has been submitted, we will process the request and provide updates via the associated ticket.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the SRCP resource request process.
- Ensure Level 3 designation is formally captured in the request.
- Do not grant access prior to completion of provisioning and security approval.
- Avoid committing to deadlines until the provisioning team confirms scheduling.

benchmark_annotation.summary: Help creating python/conda environment on CSD3

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Python / Conda Environment Management
Technical Action Required: No (user-side configuration guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (environment setup blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User attempted to create a conda environment with Python 3.12 and NumPy 2.3.5 and hit an `UnsatisfiableError`. From the visible commands, this is most likely a dependency-resolution conflict rather than a CSD3-specific failure. The safest guidance is to avoid forcing incompatible version pins, start from a clean user-managed environment, and inspect any additional requirements file if one is involved.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1) Recommend a clean user-managed Conda install if not already using one
   - Install Miniconda in `hpc-work` rather than relying on an outdated shared install.

2) Create a fresh environment with an explicit Python version, e.g.:
   - `conda create -n myenv python=3.12 -y`
   - `conda activate myenv`

3) Avoid forcing strict NumPy 2.x pins until compatibility is confirmed
   - Let Conda resolve NumPy automatically, or inspect any requirements file for packages that still expect NumPy 1.x.

4) If a `requirements.txt` is involved, review it for conflicting hard version pins before installing the full stack.

5) If the problem persists, ask for:
   - the full `requirements.txt`
   - the exact Conda commands used
   - output of `conda info` and `conda list`

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Subject: Re: Conda environment conflicts on CSD3

Hi Nico,

The error you’re seeing looks like a dependency-resolution conflict rather than a problem specific to CSD3.

We recommend creating a clean user-managed Conda environment and avoiding strict NumPy 2.x pins until the rest of the package set has been checked for compatibility. If you are using a `requirements.txt`, please review that file for conflicting version pins as well.

If the issue continues, please send us the full `requirements.txt`, the exact commands you used, and the outputs of `conda info` and `conda list`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not recommend modifying system Python.
- Do not suggest installing packages into central/shared environments.
- Avoid advising installation on compute nodes.
- Keep all installs within user-controlled directories (e.g. hpc-work).
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Python Environment Setup [1][2][3][4][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2][3][4][5]
Escalation Required: No [1][2][3][4][5]
Urgency: Medium [1][2][3][4][5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is encountering issues with creating a Python environment using conda due to conflicts with package versions. This is a common issue when using shared module environments on CSD3. The recommended approach is to install a local version of miniconda in the user's /hpc-work area to avoid these conflicts [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Advise the user to install a local version of miniconda in their /hpc-work area.
2. Provide instructions for downloading and installing miniconda from the official website.
3. Suggest using the `--prefix` option to specify the installation path in the /hpc-work area.
4. Inform the user that they will have full control over this installation and can update and change things at their leisure [1][2][3][4][5].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Nico,

The issue you are encountering is due to conflicts with package versions when using the shared module environment on CSD3. To resolve this, we recommend installing a local version of miniconda in your /hpc-work area. This will give you full control over your Python environment and avoid conflicts.

Here are the steps to install miniconda locally:

1. Download the latest version of miniconda from the official website: https://docs.anaconda.com/miniconda/#miniconda-latest-installer-links
2. Use `wget` to download the Linux x86_64 installer.
3. Run `chmod +x` to make the script executable.
4. Run the script and use the `--prefix` option to specify the installation path in your /hpc-work area.

For example:
```
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
chmod +x Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
./Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh --prefix /rds/user/nico/hpc-work/miniconda
```

Please let us know if you need further assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user installs miniconda in their /hpc-work area to avoid conflicts with the shared module environment [1][2][3][4][5].
- Do not modify the base conda installation provided by the shared module environment [1][2][3][4][5].

matplotlib

networkx

numpy

pandas

Dear Zhilin ,
Sorry you’ve been having issues with the conda module. 
Later versions of miniconda/anaconda are generally incompatible with a shared module environment. Users also do not have permission to update core components such as conda itself. You may note that our ‘latest’ version is from 2019.
We therefore advise you to download the latest version of miniconda/anaconda from their website and install it directly into your own space, in particular `~/rds/hpc-work` provides a fast and large space for all your environments. Since you will have full control over this installation you will not hit any permission issues and can update and change things at your leisure.
If you need any guidance on doing a local installation just let me know.
Henry
HPC Support

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH / MFA (TOTP)
Technical Action Required: No (user configuration issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User can successfully log into login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk but receives “Permission denied” when attempting SSH login to login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk.

User reports:
- Raven/UIS password is correct.
- MFA works for login-web.
- SSH host key accepted.
- Password recently changed.

Web login MFA and SSH MFA are separate enrolments. A common cause of this behaviour is that MFA has not yet been enrolled for SSH access (multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk), even though web MFA works.

ACTION

1) Explain SSH MFA requirement
   - Clarify that SSH requires a separate MFA enrolment from login-web.

2) Provide documentation
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

3) Instruct user to enrol SSH MFA via:

   ssh <CRSid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

   They should:
   - Enter UIS/Raven password.
   - Scan the displayed QR code into their authenticator app.
   - Confirm a new token is created (label similar to “CSD3:SSH Login”).

4) After enrolment, retry SSH to:

   ssh <CRSid>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk

5) If this does not resolve the issue:
   - Ask the user to paste the full SSH output (including prompts but excluding any codes).
   - Escalate only if MFA enrolment succeeds but authentication still fails.

EXPECTED OUTCOME
Once SSH MFA is enrolled correctly, authentication via SSH should succeed using:
- UIS/Raven password
- TOTP from the new “CSD3:SSH Login” token

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Vivek,

Although your MFA is working for login-web, SSH access requires MFA to be set up separately.

A common cause of this behaviour is that SSH MFA has not yet been enrolled. Please follow the walkthrough here:

https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

You can begin the enrolment process by running:

  ssh <CRSid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

You will be prompted for your UIS/Raven password and then shown a QR code to scan into your authenticator app.

Once this is complete, please try SSH again to:

  ssh <CRSid>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk

If the issue persists, please paste the full SSH output and we’ll investigate further.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume password error if login-web works.
- Do not reset MFA without identity verification.
- Do not suggest bypassing MFA.
- Avoid implying internal verification of MFA enrolment unless explicitly confirmed.

Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing SSH login issues despite successful web login and correct MFA setup. This is a common issue related to incorrect TOTP usage or MFA configuration [1][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if the user is using the correct TOTP code for SSH login (CSD3(SSH login)) [1][3][4].
2. Check if the user's Raven password is correct and ensure there are no keymapping issues [5].
3. If the issue persists, schedule a quick MS Teams call to reset MFA on the user's account [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm you are using the TOTP code labeled "CSD3(SSH login)" in your Microsoft Authenticator app?
- Have you recently changed your Raven password?
- Are there any non-alphanumeric characters in your Raven password that might be affected by keymapping issues?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Vivek,

Thank you for reaching out. It appears you are using the correct credentials and MFA setup, but there might be an issue with the TOTP code or password.

Please ensure you are using the TOTP code labeled "CSD3(SSH login)" in your Microsoft Authenticator app. Also, verify that your Raven password is correct and there are no keymapping issues with non-alphanumeric characters.

If the issue persists, we may need to schedule a quick MS Teams call to reset your MFA.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not perform manual MFA reset without verifying the user's identity and scheduling a call [1].
- Ensure the user is aware of the correct TOTP code to use for SSH login [1][3][4].

[3] : HPCSSUP-68747
[4] : HPCSSUP-69154
[5] : HPCSSUP-88840

benchmark_annotation.summary: Fw: Unable to submit slurm jobs on callender+

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Account & Partition Configuration
Technical Action Required: No (user-side correction)
Escalation Required: No (unless account not present in mybalance)
Urgency: Medium (job submission blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports:

  sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed:
  Invalid account or account/partition combination specified

They attempted to submit using:
  #SBATCH --account=callender+
  #SBATCH --partition=cclake

This error typically indicates one of the following:

1) The Slurm account name is incorrect (account names are case-sensitive).
2) The account name does not exactly match the allocation shown in Slurm.
3) The account/partition combination is not valid for the user.
4) The user is not using the correct Slurm account identifier format.

The correct way to determine valid accounts is via the `mybalance` command.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Ask the user to confirm available accounts
   Instruct them to run:

     mybalance

   This will display the exact account names and usage limits.

2) Confirm account string matches exactly
   - Account names are case-sensitive.
   - Hyphens and underscores are distinct.
   - Plus signs (“+”) are not normally used in Slurm account names.

3) Ensure correct SBATCH syntax
   In the submission script:

     #SBATCH --account=<EXACT_ACCOUNT_NAME_FROM_MYBALANCE>
     #SBATCH --partition=<VALID_PARTITION_FOR_ACCOUNT>

4) Validate partition compatibility
   If unsure, ask the user to provide:

     mybalance
     sinfo -s

   Confirm that:
   - The account is listed.
   - The partition is valid for that account.
   - The user is a member of that Slurm account.

5) If the account does not appear in `mybalance`
   - Verify account membership.
   - Escalate to cluster administration if necessary.

COMMON CAUSES TO CHECK

- Typographical error in account name.
- Using an informal label (e.g. project nickname) instead of the Slurm account identifier.
- Case mismatch.
- Replacing hyphens with underscores.
- Using outdated account name.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Unable to submit Slurm jobs on callender+

Hello Megha,

The error you’re seeing usually indicates that the account name or account/partition combination is not valid.

Could you please run:

  mybalance

This will show the exact Slurm account names available to you. Account names are case-sensitive and must match exactly in your submission script.

Once you have confirmed the correct account name, update your script to include:

  #SBATCH --account=<exact account name>
  #SBATCH --partition=<valid partition>

If you’re still seeing the error after confirming the account name from `mybalance`, please send the output of `mybalance` and your updated submission script so we can take a closer look.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume account misconfiguration without verifying `mybalance`.
- Do not manually alter Slurm accounts unless membership is confirmed missing.
- Avoid guessing account names; always use system-reported identifiers.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Account Configuration [1][2][3][4][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2][3][4][5]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1][2][3][4][5]
Urgency: Medium [1][2][3][4][5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is unable to submit SLURM jobs due to an "Invalid account or account/partition combination" error. This issue is commonly resolved by ensuring the user has the correct permissions and partitions associated with their account [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's account (callender+) and associated partitions.
2. Check if the account callender+ has the necessary partitions (e.g., cclake, icelake, skylake) attached.
3. If the partitions are missing, add the required partitions to the user's account.
4. Confirm with the PI (Dr. Callender) if any special permissions or partitions are required for the account.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact SLURM job submission script you are using?
- Have you tried submitting jobs to other partitions (e.g., desktop, cclake, icelake)?
- Are there any specific partitions that your PI has mentioned should be available under callender+?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Megha,

Thank you for your message. The issue you're experiencing is likely due to missing partitions associated with your account (callender+). We will verify the account and add the necessary partitions if they are missing.

Please provide the exact SLURM job submission script you are using, and let us know if there are any specific partitions that your PI has mentioned should be available under callender+.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any changes to account permissions are approved by the PI or an authorized representative [3][5].
- Do not grant access to partitions without proper verification and approval.

[1] : HPCSSUP-47649
[2] : HPCSSUP-83465
[3] : HPCSSUP-32754
[4] : HPCSSUP-41812
[5] : HPCSSUP-78317
metrics.user_input:
Hi,
I can’t submit any Slurm jobs using the Callender+ account, PI Dr Callender copied in,  because it doesn’t seem to have any partitions attached to it, so every sbatch call fails with an “Invalid account or account/partition combination” error. I can install packages on the login node, but can’t see that my environment works end-to-end without running jobs on compute nodes. I would be grateful for your advice on whether a CPU partition is available under callender+ and how to resolve this please?
Best wishes,
Megha

[SUMMARY] cannot submit SL3 CPU job: invalid account or account/partition combination - dj346

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
To whom it may concern,  
I am Dongcheng Jiang and my HPC username (CRSid) is dj346. I tried to follow the guidance on submitting array jobs ([https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/batch.html#array-jobs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/batch.html#array-jobs)). Using the command mybalance, I can see 4 accounts: WOODLAND-SL2-CPU, WOODLAND-SL3-CPU, WOODLAND-SL2-GPU, WOODLAND-SL3-GPU, and I believe I should be able to submit SL3 CPU jobs.  
This is my submission script: /rds/user/dj346/hpc-work/espnet/egs/an4/asr1/submit_array_test  
When I tried to submit the jobs via the command sbatch submit_array_test (I was already under the directory /rds/user/dj346/hpc-work/espnet/egs/an4/asr1/), the following error was reported: 
 
sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account or account/partition combination specified  
I also investigated the problem by trying to start interactive SL3 CPU sessions using this command: 
sintr -A WOODLAND-SL3-CPU -p cclake -N1 -n1 -t 1:0:0 --qos=INTR
, and the same error message occurred. I tried cclake, icelake, skylake for -p option, but the same error occurred repeatedly. 
 
So could you please help me find out what was going wrong with my SL3 CPU submission? Thank you very much and I am looking forward to your reply. 
 
Best wishes, 
Dongcheng

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Dongcheng,
I can reproduce this error with your account, so there does appear to be something incorrect in how it was setup. I haven't found the reason yet. I'll come back to you shortly once I have an update.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Dongcheng,
That appears to be fixed now, your account was correctly setup but there was a wider issue that required a service to be restarted.
I've checked that your account is able to submit jobs to WOODLAND-SL3-CPU now, so I'll close the ticket. If you still have any problems, reply back to reopen it and we can take a look.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

benchmark_annotation.summary: Adding student collaborators to RDS disk and supervisor's accounts

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Membership / Slurm Account Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (conditional – Slurm account membership)
Portal Action Required: Yes (RDS membership via Self Service)
Escalation Required: Conditional (PI approval required for Slurm accounts)
Urgency: Low–Medium (collaborator onboarding)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests:

1) Add two students to an RDS storage project:
   rds-posecraft-5SVx1x5hoTY

2) Add the same users to multiple Slurm accounts:
   OZTIRELI-SL2-CPU
   OZTIRELI-SL2-GPU
   OZTIRELI-SL3-CPU
   OZTIRELI-SL3-GPU

Key principles:

- RDS project membership is managed by the Data Owner or Data Manager via the Self Service portal. HPC Support does not directly administer storage memberships.
- Slurm account access can only be granted with explicit approval from the PI/account owner.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) RDS Membership
   - Inform user that storage project membership is controlled via the Self Service portal.
   - Direct them to confirm that the Data Owner or Data Manager has added the students.
   - If necessary, advise them to check:
       https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/

2) Slurm Account Access
   - Confirm whether explicit approval from the account owner (PI) has been provided.
   - If not, request written approval from the PI before proceeding.
   - Once approval is received, add the users to the specified Slurm accounts.

3) Do NOT:
   - Add users to Slurm accounts without PI approval.
   - Modify storage memberships manually.
   - Assume requester has authority unless they are confirmed Data Owner/PI.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (IF APPROVAL NOT CLEAR)

- Are you the Data Owner or Data Manager of the RDS project?
- Can the PI/account owner confirm approval for adding these users to the Slurm accounts?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Adding collaborators to RDS and Slurm accounts

Dear Zhilin,

Regarding the RDS project, storage memberships are managed by the Data Owner or Data Manager via the Self Service portal. We do not directly administer storage project memberships. Please ensure the relevant Data Owner/Data Manager has added the students through:

  https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/

For the Slurm accounts, we can add the students once we have explicit approval from the account owner (PI). If this has not yet been provided, could the PI please confirm approval?

Once confirmed, we will proceed accordingly.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Storage access must follow Data Owner governance.
- Slurm account additions require PI approval.
- Do not bypass project ownership controls.
- Maintain auditability of account access changes.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: AIRR / Authentication / Keycloak (External User)
Technical Action Required: Yes (local Keycloak account creation)
Escalation Required: Yes (AIRR team)
Urgency: Medium (invitation expiry deadline)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User has been invited to join the “UKAEA Early Adopters” project on AIRR and requests creation of a local Keycloak account.

Key points:
- User is external (UKAEA email).
- Invitation link has an expiry date.
- “Local Keycloak entry” indicates use of the documented “without MyAccessID (last resort)” workflow.
- Account must be created in Keycloak before the user can accept the AIRR invitation via “Sign in with Keycloak”.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm invitation context (internal)
   - Verify invitation exists for the user email.
   - Confirm project name: “UKAEA Early Adopters”.
   - Confirm partition/access level requested.

2) Escalate to AIRR team (if not directly handled by HPC Support)
   - Forward ticket to AIRR administrators.
   - Include:
       • Full name
       • Organisation
       • Email
       • Project/partition name
   - Mark urgency if invitation expiry is near.

3) Create local Keycloak account (AIRR/admin action)
   - Create account using provided email.
   - Trigger password setup email.
   - Ensure account is linked to the appropriate realm.

4) Notify user once action is underway
   - Explain that once the local account has been created, they will receive a password setup email.
   - Advise checking spam/junk folders.
   - Instruct them to:
       a) Set password,
       b) Visit invitation link,
       c) Choose “Sign in with Keycloak”,
       d) Use newly created credentials.

5) Provide documentation link
   - Reference official guidance for “Accessing without MyAccessID (last resort)”.

6) Do NOT:
   - Manually approve the project membership unless that is part of the AIRR workflow.
   - Ask for passwords.
   - Override the invitation process.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Has the invitation expired?
- Have you received the password setup email?
- Are you able to access the invitation link successfully?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: UKAEA Early Adopters – AIRR Access

Hi Waqar,

We will arrange creation of the local Keycloak account needed for this AIRR invitation.

Once the account has been created, you should receive an email to set your password. After that, please visit the invitation link, select “Sign in with Keycloak”, and log in using those credentials.

For reference, please see:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#accessing-without-myaccessid-last-resort

Please let us know if you encounter any issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not transmit passwords via email.
- Do not request sensitive credentials.
- Ensure external accounts are created only through the approved Keycloak workflow.
- Respect invitation expiry deadlines but do not bypass approval controls.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / GPU Scheduling / Walltime Limits
Technical Action Required: No (policy explanation + workflow guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (jobs being cancelled due to time limit)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is concerned that GPU jobs cannot exceed the 36-hour walltime limit and asks how to make best use of the allocation.

Key principles:
- GPU partitions enforce a maximum walltime limit (e.g. 36 hours).
- This is a scheduler policy limit, not a configuration error.
- The limit applies regardless of remaining budgeted GPU hours.
- Allocated GPU hours (account balance) are separate from maximum walltime per job.
- Long-running workflows should use checkpointing and job chaining.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm policy limit
   - Explain that the 36-hour walltime is an enforced scheduler limit.
   - It cannot be overridden per job at the user level.

2) Clarify allocation model
   - GPU hour budget defines total consumption allowed.
   - Walltime limit defines maximum duration of a single job.
   - Having remaining hours does not permit exceeding the per-job limit.

3) Recommend checkpointing (best practice)
   - Modify code to periodically write restart/checkpoint files.
   - Submit jobs so that they resume from the last checkpoint.
   - Use job arrays or chained submissions to continue computation.

4) Optional: suggest job chaining
   - Use dependent jobs:
       sbatch --dependency=afterok:<jobid> next_job.sh
   - Or include automatic resubmission logic if checkpoint detected.

6) Do NOT:
   - Promise walltime extensions.
   - Modify partition policy.
   - Suggest splitting allocation across multiple concurrent long jobs to bypass limits.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (IF NEEDED)
- Which partition/account are you using?
- How long does your full workflow need to run?
- Does your code support checkpoint/restart?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Support for job submissions

Hi Sam,

The 36-hour walltime limit is a scheduler policy on the GPU partition and cannot be extended for individual jobs. Even if you have sufficient GPU hours remaining in your allocation, the maximum runtime per job is still capped.

To run longer workflows, we recommend implementing checkpointing so that your job can resume from the last saved state once the walltime is reached. You can then submit follow-up jobs to continue from the checkpoint.

Please let us know if you would like guidance on setting up checkpointing or job dependencies.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Walltime limits are enforced at scheduler level.
- Allocation balance does not override per-job limits.
- Avoid suggesting policy circumvention.
- Promote checkpoint/restart as standard HPC best practice.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / SLURM Configuration [1][2][3][4][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Sam is encountering limitations with the 36-hour wall time and the maximum number of concurrent jobs for GPU allocations. The user also needs guidance on SLURM script settings for non-GPU jobs.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the user's account type and allocation details.
2. Provide guidance on using array jobs to manage the maximum number of concurrent jobs.
3. Advise on SLURM script settings for non-GPU jobs.

ACTION
Dear Sam,

To maximize your GPU allocation, you can use array jobs to manage the maximum number of concurrent jobs. Here is an example of how to set up your SLURM script for array jobs:

```
#SBATCH --array=0-99%32
#SBATCH --time=35:30:00
```

This configuration will allow up to 32 jobs to run concurrently, with the next job starting only after one of the previous jobs has finished.

For non-GPU jobs, you can omit the `--gres=gpu:1` directive from your SLURM script. Here is an example:

```
#SBATCH --array=0-99
#SBATCH --time=35:30:00
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=4G
```

Please let us know if you have any further questions or need additional assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's account type and allocation details are confirmed before providing specific guidance.
- Do not exceed the maximum wall time and concurrent job limits specified for the user's account type.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-62385
[2] : HPCSSUP-93640
[3] : HPCSSUP-69755
[4] : HPCSSUP-80567
[5] : HPCSSUP-35991

The 36-hour walltime limit is a scheduler policy on the GPU partition and cannot be extended for individual jobs. Even if you have sufficient GPU hours remaining in your allocation, the maximum runtime per job is still capped.

To run longer workflows, we recommend implementing checkpointing so that your job can resume from the last saved state once the walltime is reached. You can then submit follow-up jobs to continue from the checkpoint.

Please let us know if you would like guidance on setting up checkpointing or job dependencies.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Walltime limits are enforced at scheduler level.
- Allocation balance does not override per-job limits.
- Avoid suggesting policy circumvention.
- Promote checkpoint/restart as standard HPC best practice.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the user's account type and allocation details.

ACTION
Dear Sam,

To maximize your GPU allocation, you can use array jobs to manage the maximum number of concurrent jobs. Here is an example of how to set up your SLURM script for array jobs:

```
#SBATCH --array=0-99%32
#SBATCH --time=35:30:00
```

This configuration will allow up to 32 jobs to run concurrently, with the next job starting only after one of the previous jobs has finished.

For non-GPU jobs, you can omit the `--gres=gpu:1` directive from your SLURM script. Here is an example:

```
#SBATCH --array=0-99
#SBATCH --time=35:30:00
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --mem=4G
```

Please let us know if you have any further questions or need additional assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Ensure that the user's account type and allocation details are confirmed before providing specific guidance.

benchmark_annotation.summary: Q about how to request CPUs on Wilkes

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / GPU-CPU Resource Ratios / Wilkes3 (Ampere)
Technical Action Required: No (scheduler policy clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is running on Wilkes3 (Ampere) requesting:
- 1 node
- 1 GPU
- 1 task
- Wants to increase CPUs from 32 to 76 while still using only 1 GPU.

On Wilkes3 Ampere nodes, Slurm enforces a CPU-per-GPU ratio:
- Maximum 32 CPUs per GPU.
- This is a scheduler-level constraint.
- Users cannot request arbitrary CPU counts independent of GPU count.

Therefore:
- 1 GPU → maximum 32 CPUs.
- To request ~76 CPUs, the user must request 3 GPUs (32 × 3 = 96 CPUs maximum available).

The scheduler will not allow 76 CPUs with only 1 GPU.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Explain enforced policy
   - Ampere nodes allocate CPUs in proportion to GPUs.
   - 32 CPUs per GPU maximum.
   - This prevents GPU starvation and ensures fair resource use.

2) Calculate requirement
   - 76 CPUs ÷ 32 CPUs per GPU ≈ 2.38 GPUs.
   - Since GPUs are indivisible, user must request 3 GPUs.

3) Provide correct submission guidance
   Example directives:
     #SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
     #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=76
   (Ensure CPUs requested do not exceed 32 × GPUs requested.)

4) Clarify limitations
   - Cannot exceed enforced CPU-per-GPU ratio.
   - Cannot request fractional GPUs.
   - Cannot override scheduler policy.

5) Do NOT:
   - Suggest bypassing CPU limits.
   - Recommend modifying system configuration.
   - Escalate unless a policy exception is formally requested.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Which Wilkes partition are you using?
- Are you using --ntasks or --cpus-per-task?
- Do you require 76 CPUs per task or across tasks?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: CPU request on Wilkes

Hello Mel,

On Wilkes3 (Ampere), Slurm enforces a maximum of 32 CPUs per GPU to avoid starving GPUs of CPU resources. This means that if you request 1 GPU, you can receive at most 32 CPUs.

To obtain approximately 76 CPUs, you would need to request 3 GPUs (since 32 × 3 = 96 CPUs available). GPUs must be requested in whole numbers, so fractional allocations are not possible.

For example:

  #SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
  #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=76

Please let us know if you need help adjusting your submission script.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- CPU-per-GPU ratio is scheduler-enforced.
- Do not imply the limit can be overridden.
- Avoid suggesting inefficient resource usage beyond policy.
- Ensure guidance aligns with documented Wilkes3 resource allocation rules.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Job Submission
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants to increase the number of CPUs used in a job on Wilkes from 32 to 76 while using 1 GPU. The retrieved documents indicate that the Wilkes2 nodes have 12 cores each, and the Wilkes3 nodes have 2 x AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors [1][3]. The user can increase the number of CPUs by adjusting the `--ntasks` parameter in the job submission script [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm with the user that they are using the Wilkes3 partition, as it has nodes with 128 cores (2 x 64-Core processors) [1][3].
2. Instruct the user to modify the job submission script to set `--ntasks=76` to allocate 76 CPUs [3].
3. Ensure the user retains the `--gres=gpu:1` parameter to allocate 1 GPU [3].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Mel,

To increase the number of CPUs to 76 while using 1 GPU, you can modify your job submission script as follows:

```sh
#!/bin/sh
#SBATCH -J your_job_name
#SBATCH -A your_account
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=76
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time=your_time_limit
#SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL
#SBATCH --partition ampere
```

Please ensure you are using the Wilkes3 partition, as it has nodes with 128 cores (2 x 64-Core processors).

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is aware of the resource limits and costs associated with their job submission [1][3].
- Do not manually adjust job parameters without user confirmation.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Job Submission
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants to increase the number of CPUs used in a job on Wilkes from 32 to 76 while using 1 GPU. The retrieved documents indicate that the Wilkes2 nodes have 12 cores each, and the Wilkes3 nodes have 2 x AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors [1][3]. The user can increase the number of CPUs by adjusting the `--ntasks` parameter in the job submission script [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm with the user that they are using the Wilkes3 partition, as it has nodes with 128 cores (2 x 64-Core processors) [1][3].
2. Instruct the user to modify the job submission script to set `--ntasks=76` to allocate 76 CPUs [3].
3. Ensure the user retains the `--gres=gpu:1` parameter to allocate 1 GPU [3].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Mel,

To increase the number of CPUs to 76 while using 1 GPU, you can modify your job submission script as follows:

```sh
#!/bin/sh
#SBATCH -J your_job_name
#SBATCH -A your_account
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=76
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time=your_time_limit
#SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL
#SBATCH --partition ampere
```

Please ensure you are using the Wilkes3 partition, as it has nodes with 128 cores (2 x 64-Core processors).

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is aware of the resource limits and costs associated with their job submission [1][3].
- Do not manually adjust job parameters without user confirmation.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-48230
[3] : HPCSSUP-66798

[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-22949
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2020-02-06T17:10:12.213+0000
[SUMMARY] CPU cores in gpu jobs

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support,
I'm doing computations on Wilkes2, at the moment with jobs on only one 
GPU each. Since there are 12 core CPUs equiped in each node, do I 
automatically get 3 cpu nodes when booking a job with 1 gpu? I'm using 
the python multiprocessing toolbox to load the next data batch in ram 
while training on the GPU.
Thanks,
Thomas

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Thomas,
Yes you are forced (by our customised job submission filter) to request 3 cpu cores per GPU. If you try to do something different from this then the submission should fail; if you don't specify cpus at all then this is what you will get. The filter does insist that you specify the number of GPUs per node.
SLURM will then also give 1/4 of the node RAM (i.e. ordinary server memory, not GPU memory) per GPU. If you need more, you will need to increase the fraction of the node you are allocated, up to the full 96GB of host memory, by asking for more of the 4 gpus.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

<MESSAGE id=0008 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Most likely, you are running out of memory when you do that. I have a theory as to why this is - this is your current job:
```
sacct -j 53636475 --format=alloctres'%45',nodelist'%45' -X
                                    AllocTRES                                      NodeList 
--------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------- 
         billing=15,cpu=15,mem=51300M,node=15 cpu-p-[55,66,70-73,82,194,203,281,283-284,28+ 

```
You’ve asked for 15 cpus and 15 nodes, but your jupyter kernel will only be running on the first node (cpu-p-55). The other 14 nodes are being wasted, they are not doing anything, because jupyter only supports the type of parallelism that can use cpus from one node.
Moreover, the memory allocated is distributed evenly with the cpus, and since you cannot allocate a node without allocating at least 1 cpu from that node, each of the 15 nodes has just 1 cpu allocated to your job, and with it just 3420MB of memory (15*3420MB=51300MB). Consequently, your jupyter kernel has only 3420MB available to it, which is probably too little.
Both of these issues will be resolved if you change the size of the job request to cpus=15, nodes=1. Then 15 cpus on the same node will become available to your kernel, and correspondingly 15 times the amount of memory. This will cost the same to your project as the original job, but will be using all the resources allocated. You can increase 15 up to 56 on the cclake nodes and get proportionately more memory assigned to your jupyter kernel, at proportionately greater cost.
Please try that and see if it solves the problem.
Regards
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RFS / Access Method
Technical Action Required: No (guidance + possible storage conversion request)
Escalation Required: Yes (if user requests NFS-RFS conversion)
Urgency: Low–Medium (workflow clarification)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is attempting to locate an RFS (Research File Share) project under /rfs/project/ on HPC and assumes it should be accessible similarly to RDS. This is incorrect.

Standard RFS storage:
- Is not directly mounted on CSD3/HPC compute or login nodes.
- Is accessed via OS-level mounting from institutional machines (SMB on Windows, SMB/NFS on macOS/Linux).
- Cannot be accessed through the HPC filesystem unless converted to NFS-RFS.

User’s intended workflow:
- Active work on RDS
- Backed-up storage on RFS
- Copy between RDS and RFS

This requires clarification of storage models and possibly a request to convert the RFS project to NFS-RFS.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Clarify storage separation
   Explain that:
   - RDS = high-performance Lustre storage mounted on HPC under /rds
   - RFS = Research File Share (backed-up institutional storage)
   - Standard RFS is not accessible from HPC

2) Answer direct question
   The path does not exist on HPC because RFS is not mounted there.
   It must be accessed via:
   - Windows/macOS mount instructions
   - SMB/NFS mount from a workstation

3) Provide supported HPC-access option
   If user needs RFS accessible from HPC, instruct:
   - Request conversion to NFS-RFS
   - Provide documentation link (RFS on CSD3 – NFS-RFS)
   - Clarify that NFS-RFS allows HPC-side mounting but may not offer RDS-level performance

4) Clarify common workflow
   Typical pattern:
   - Active datasets → RDS
   - Backed-up archival data → RFS or RCS
   - Copy data between systems as required
   - NFS-RFS if active + backed-up + HPC access needed

5) If user confirms NFS-RFS is desired
   - Ask them to email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk with:
       Project name
       Project ID
       Confirmation they want RFS converted to NFS-RFS
   - Escalate to storage team

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Do you require the RFS space to be mounted directly on HPC?
- Is this for active HPC workflows or archival/backup only?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Path to RFS storage

Hello Heather,

RFS storage is not directly mounted on the HPC filesystem, which is why you cannot find it under /rfs/project/.

Standard RFS is accessed by mounting it from your local machine (e.g. via SMB). It is not available on CSD3 login or compute nodes by default.

If you require your RFS project to be accessible from HPC, you can request that it be converted to NFS-RFS. This allows it to be mounted on CSD3, although performance characteristics differ from RDS.

Please see:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/rfs-nfs.html

If you would like to proceed with conversion to NFS-RFS, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk with the project name and ID and we can arrange this for you.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply RFS is mounted on HPC by default.
- Do not suggest manual mounting inside compute jobs.
- Do not guarantee performance equivalence between RDS and NFS-RFS.
- Escalate only if conversion request is explicitly confirmed.

User is attempting to locate an RFS (Research File Share) project under /rfs/project/ on HPC and assumes it should be accessible similarly to RDS. This is incorrect.

Standard RFS storage:
- Is not directly mounted on CSD3/HPC compute or login nodes.
- Is accessed via OS-level mounting from institutional machines (SMB on Windows, SMB/NFS on macOS/Linux).
- Cannot be accessed through the HPC filesystem unless converted to NFS-RFS.

User’s intended workflow:
- Active work on RDS
- Backed-up storage on RFS
- Copy between RDS and RFS

1) Clarify storage separation
   Explain that:
   - RDS = high-performance Lustre storage mounted on HPC under /rds
   - RFS = Research File Share (backed-up institutional storage)
   - Standard RFS is not accessible from HPC

2) Answer direct question
   The path does not exist on HPC because RFS is not mounted there.
   It must be accessed via:
   - Windows/macOS mount instructions
   - SMB/NFS mount from a workstation

3) Provide supported HPC-access option
   If user needs RFS accessible from HPC, instruct:
   - Request conversion to NFS-RFS
   - Provide documentation link (RFS on CSD3 – NFS-RFS)
   - Clarify that NFS-RFS allows HPC-side mounting but may not offer RDS-level performance

4) Clarify common workflow
   Typical pattern:
   - Active datasets → RDS
   - Backed-up archival data → RFS or RCS
   - Copy data between systems as required
   - NFS-RFS if active + backed-up + HPC access needed
