QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is a "FATAL: permission denied" error when attempting to run the AlphaFold3 Singularity container. This error has been encountered by other users and has been resolved by ensuring the correct paths and permissions are set [1][2][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the exact commands and paths used by Daniel.
2. Verify the permissions of the `run_alphafold3` script and the Singularity container.
3. Check if the `INSTALL_DIR` and `APPTAINER_TMPDIR` paths are correctly set and accessible.
4. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher-level support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide the exact commands you used to install and run the container?
- Can you confirm the permissions of the `run_alphafold3` script and the Singularity container?
- Can you run `ls -l /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3` and `ls -l /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/ap3_apptainer_tmp` to check the paths?

Dear HPC Support Team,

I was wondering whether you know whether we need to reach out to Google DeepMind directly (as described here: [https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3?tab=readme-ov-file](https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3?tab=readme-ov-file)) to obtain the model parameters, or whether there might be already be a procedure set up for Cambridge groups to obtain access to a shared existing copy of the weights on the HPC in a way that's consistent with the terms of use.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Question about Running Alphafold3 Singularity Container on the HPC

[CONTEXT]
Dear Elisabeth,
Just wondering whether you have had a chance to look into this?
Many thanks,
Daniel

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Daniel,
Can I confirm that you have done the following in your terminal?

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3
cat <<EOF> run_alphafold3
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif 
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \\
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```
This should create `run_alphafold3` which should work with the gpus.
Best regards,
Elisabeth

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Daniel,
Do you require support on this still? If not, I will close this ticket.
Best regards,
Elisabeth Reeve

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>

"'s at the end of the "B .:/etc" and "-db_dir=/data" lines). This created the "/home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3/run_alphafold3" script which I am using to try to the run the container in my slurm script.
Thanks,
Danny

</MESSAGE>

The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking assistance with compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3, specifically on NVIDIA nodes. There is a resolved ticket (HPCSSUP-90945) that provides detailed steps and module commands for compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3, which can be used as a reference.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the resolved ticket HPCSSUP-90945 for detailed instructions on compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3 [1].
2. Provide the user with the module commands and arch.mk file adjustments as outlined in the resolved ticket [1].
3. If the user encounters further issues, escalate to the RSE team for additional support.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Bingcan,

Thank you for your message. We have a resolved ticket that provides detailed instructions for compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3. Please follow these steps:

1. Run the following module commands:
```
module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base
module load intel-oneapi-compilers/2023.2.4/gcc/4lbvg4hv
module load hdf5/1.14.3/intel/intel-oneapi-mpi/si2tlq4v
module load intel-oneapi-mkl-cluster/2024.1.0/intel/r55ddo3z
```
2. Use the attached arch.mk file provided in the resolved ticket.
3. Ensure you run the above module commands from your job script when using the produced binaries.

If you encounter any further issues, please let us know, and we will escalate your request to our RSE team for additional support.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user follows the exact module commands and arch.mk file adjustments to avoid compilation issues.
- Escalate to the RSE team if the user encounters persistent problems.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-90945

[SUMMARY] Build/Compilation problems with BerkeleyGW on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support team,
I hope you are well. I’m currently trying to build and compile the BerkeleyGW 4.0 ([http://manual.berkeleygw.org/4.0/compilation/](http://manual.berkeleygw.org/4.0/compilation/)) code on CSD3 to perform atomistic simulations, but I’m currently facing problems related to the linking of libraries and modules required for the compilation of the program.
BerkeleyGW is built by running a arch.mk file that is architecture dependent, connecting the different required libraries. I’ve included the base file that is the closest to the architecture used on CSD3:
——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
COMPFLAG = -DINTEL
PARAFLAG = -DMPI -DOMP
MATHFLAG = -DUSESCALAPACK -DUNPACKED -DUSEFFTW3 -DHDF5 -DUSEMR3 # -DUSEPRIMME
1. Only uncomment DEBUGFLAG if you need to develop/debug BerkeleyGW.
2. The output will be much more verbose, and the code will slow down by ~20%.
#DEBUGFLAG = -DDEBUG
#########################################################################
1. NOTE: This arch.mk<[http://arch.mk/](http://arch.mk/)> is used by the testsuite. The compiler flags are #
2. optimized to debug the code and not for code performance.      #
#########################################################################
FCPP  = /usr/bin/cpp -C -nostdinc # -ansi# Intel oneAPI Compiler Options
F90free = mpiifort -free -O3 -qopenmp -g -traceback
LINK  = mpiifort -free -O3 -qopenmp -g -traceback
FOPTS  = -O3
FNOOPTS = $(FOPTS)
MOD_OPT = -module ./
INCFLAG = -I# Intel oneAPI C Compiler Options
CC_COMP = mpiicpc -qopenmp
C_COMP  = mpiicc -qopenmp
C_LINK  = mpiicc -qopenmp
C_OPTS  = -O3
C_DEBUGFLAG =REMOVE = /bin/rm -f# Math Libraries
#MKLROOT = /opt/intel/oneapi/mkl/latest
FFTWLIB   = $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_scalapack_lp64.a Wl,-start-group $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_intel_lp64.a \
        $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_intel_thread.a $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_core.a $(MKLROOT)/lib/intel64/libmkl_blacs_intelmpi_lp64.a \
        Wl,-end-group -lpthread -lm
FFTWINCLUDE = $(MKLROOT)/include/fftw/LAPACKLIB = $(FFTWLIB)# HDF5 Library Options
HDF5_LDIR  = /usr/local/Cluster-Apps/hdf5/mpi/intel/2019.3/1.10.5/lib/
HDF5LIB   = $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5hl_fortran.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5_hl.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5_fortran.a \
        $(HDF5_LDIR)/libhdf5.a -lz -ldl
HDF5INCLUDE = /usr/local/Cluster-Apps/hdf5/mpi/intel/2019.3/1.10.5/include
TESTSCRIPT =
——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
I’ve correctly compiled this before locally on a Linux maxhine with OpenMPI and the arch.mk file looked like this, so the end result should be the same:
——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
COMPFLAG  = -DGNU
PARAFLAG  = -DMPI
MATHFLAG  = -DUSESCALAPACK -DHDF5
1. Only uncomment DEBUGFLAG if you need to develop/debug BerkeleyGW.
1. The output will be much more verbose, and the code will slow down by ~20%.
#DEBUGFLAG = -DDEBUG
#########################################################################
1. NOTE: This arch.mk is used by a buildslave. The compiler flags are  #
1. optimized to debug the code and not for code performance.           #
#########################################################################
FCPP    = cpp -C -nostdinc -nostdinc++
#F90free = /usr/bin/mpif90 -ffree-form -ffree-line-length-none -Wall
F90free = /usr/bin/mpif90 -ffree-form -ffree-line-length-none -fbounds-check -Wall -pedantic-errors -std=gnu

Looking forward to hear back from you.

Kem

Hi Jeffrey,

./configure --prefix=${HOME}/QE  MPIF90=mpifort \

</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
That should be a fine start. We prefer the ELPA libraries (with --with-elpa options to tell it where elpa is installed) for large-scale problems but it generally doesn't make a great deal of difference. 
Let me know if you run into errors and we will do our best to help. 
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
When I try this configuration, I get some warnings:
configure: WARNING: F90 value is set to be consistent with value of MPIF90
checking for mpifort... no
configure: WARNING: MPIF90 not found: using MPIF90 anyway
checking version of mpifort... unknown, assuming gfortran
checking for Fortran flag to compile .f90 files... unknown
configure: error: Fortran could not compile .f90 files
Should I change the MPIF90 option to something else?
Best,
Kem
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Ah yes, `mpifort` is not a wrapper provided by Intel MPI. Unfortunately there is no consensus on what fortran compilers should be called - fort, f90, fc are all used. In this case you can use mpif90 rather than mpifort.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

benchmark_annotation.summary: Change interactive Rstudio R version to 4.5.1

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / R / RStudio [1][2][3][4]
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants to use a newer version of R (4.5.1) in the interactive RStudio session. The available R versions and their compatibility with RStudio need to be checked.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the available R versions by running `module av R` on the login node [1][2][3].
2. Check if R/4.5.1 is available or if a similar version can be used.
3. If R/4.5.1 is not available, suggest the closest available version and provide instructions on how to load it.
4. Provide instructions on how to load the RStudio module and start an interactive session [4].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Heather,

Thank you for your request. To use a newer version of R in the interactive RStudio session, please follow these steps:

1. Log in to the HPC system.
2. Check the available R versions by running `module av R`.
3. If R/4.5.1 is available, load it using `module load R/4.5.1`. If not, load the closest available version.
4. Load the RStudio module using `module load RStudio/1.0.143`.
5. Start an interactive RStudio session by running `rstudio`.

Please let us know if you encounter any issues or need further assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is aware of the available R versions and how to load them correctly.
- Do not install new R versions without proper authorization and testing.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-101871
[2] : HPCSSUP-50288
[3] : HPCSSUP-19531
[4] : HPCSSUP-21984

benchmark_annotation.summary: Installing level-zero for multi-xpu training with Intel XPU

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Installation [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2]
Escalation Required: Yes [1][2]
Urgency: Medium [1][2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting assistance with installing the `level-zero-dev` package to resolve an error related to multi-xpu fine-tuning on DAWN Intel XPUs. The user does not have sudo authentication to install the package themselves. Previous tickets indicate that similar issues have been resolved by the RSE team, and there is a precedent for handling such requests [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the ticket to the RSE team for further investigation and resolution [1][2].
2. Inform the user that the request has been escalated and that the RSE team will be in touch with further instructions or a resolution.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Jiangnan,

Thank you for your request. We have escalated your ticket to the RSE team for further investigation and resolution. They will be in touch with you shortly with further instructions or a resolution.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not attempt to install packages requiring sudo authentication without proper authorization [1][2].
- Escalate requests involving package installations to the RSE team for review and action [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-69227
[2] : HPCSSUP-71231
metrics.user_input:
Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

pip3 install --user mappy==2.22
 
conda install -c bioconda mappy
 
git clone [https://github.com/lh3/minimap2](https://github.com/lh3/minimap2)
cd minimap2
python setup.py install --user

Leon Bond commented:
Dear Maja,
I believe that the version of blast we have on the cluster is too old (v2.4) to make secure connections to the remote site, i.e. https:// instead of http://
The blast documentation sugggests that v2.5+ is needed for this ([1][https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/))
We've built a v2.9 module of blast, which you can try by running
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr 
That isn't currently visible to users by default, as we've had to use a different version of the 'spack' software packaging tool for this. In the near future we'll be making changes that will allow this to be visible via a search.
Could you try using this module and see if it works for you instead of blast/2.4.0 ?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

output="/home/mn480/rds/hpc-work/blastn_out_array"

benchmark_annotation.summary: Conda environment not activating correctly in Slurm jobs (uses base Python instead of env)

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Conda / SLURM Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with activating a Conda environment within a SLURM batch job. The environment activates correctly on the login node but not within the SLURM job, which defaults to the base Conda Python interpreter.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's SLURM script to ensure it includes the necessary commands to source the `.bashrc` file and activate the Conda environment.
2. Ensure the user is not using `conda init` within the SLURM script, as this is not required and can cause issues [2][3][4].
3. Confirm the user is loading the `miniconda` module before activating the Conda environment [1][2][3][4][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact SLURM script you are using?
- Have you tried adding `source ~/.bashrc` before the `conda activate ml` command in your SLURM script?
- Are there any specific error messages in the SLURM output files (`conda_test.out` or `conda_test.err`)?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stefanos,

To ensure your Conda environment is activated correctly within your SLURM batch job, please make the following changes to your SLURM script:

1. Load the `miniconda` module.
2. Source your `.bashrc` file.
3. Activate your Conda environment.

Here is an example of how your SLURM script should look:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load the miniconda module
module load miniconda

# Source the .bashrc file
source ~/.bashrc

# Activate the environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

Please try this and let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is not using `conda init` within the SLURM script, as this is not required and can cause issues [2][3][4].
- Confirm the user is loading the `miniconda` module before activating the Conda environment [1][2][3][4][5].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-16898
[2] : HPCSSUP-52206
[3] : HPCSSUP-65220
[4] : HPCSSUP-26914
[5] : HPCSSUP-83117
metrics.user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos

On the logging node, I would run: 
> module load miniconda3-4.5.4-gcc-5.4.0-hivczbz

But when I put that in the SLURM file, the environment is not loaded and the job fails because of missing packages.

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Akshay,
The order of the commands to activate your environment should be:
```java
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh                # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge                               # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-icl              # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed: 
module load python/3.8 
module load miniconda/3

conda init --all 
source /home/aj686/.bashrc
conda activate euclid
```
Please change your submission script and try again. Let me know if this worked for you.
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003a role=TICKET_CREATOR part=1/2 original_id=0003>
Dear Avraam,
Thank you for your suggestion. It doesn't work though, yielding the same error as before,i.e, numpy not found. The environment activates perfectly ok on the login node. I also tried using source activate instead of conda activate. That doesn't work either.
Akshay

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
I'm currently trying to get some code running when I submit it to slurm on CSD3.

/home/crhw3/batch_tests/submit_eor_job.sh

run the line of code, e.g. using:

And the code works. However when I put this in a slurm script, I get an error. The error is as follows:

To initialize your shell, run

Charlie

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Activate specific conda environment - run jobs

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
It seems that I cannot activate my conda environment in my jobs. I launch a non-interactive job on CSD3 with the script attached but when it comes to run the python file I got the error : "ModuleNotFoundError" as if the activation of my environment did not work.
Yet I specified  
" 
module load python/3.6

source activate  myenv"
in the slurm file and still get "ModuleNotFoundError" as output (it does not find the torch module).
Could you help me please ?
Best regards, 
Matthieu

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Activating conda environment 

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support Team,
Hope this email finds you well.
I am writing to ask for help when activating a conda environment inside a SLURM job. I have submitted multiple jobs with the following script (activating the conda environment using `source activate`):
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J castep-xyz
#SBATCH -A MOYA-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=28
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH -p icelake
#! Output filename:
#! %A means slurm job ID and %a means array index
#SBATCH --output=castep_alpha_1_%A.out
#! Errors filename:
#SBATCH --error=castep_alpha_1_%A.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#! ###############################
#! Script to run CASTEP from ASE
#! ###############################
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-icl # Loads the basic environment
module load castep/impi/22.11 # Load CASTEP
module load miniconda/3 # Load miniconda, required otherwise cannot activate conda env
source activate /home/jiq21/.conda/envs/conv
export I_MPI_PIN_DOMAIN=omp:compact
export I_MPI_PIN_ORDER=scatter
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e 's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 32:
#export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
ARRAYID=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID, ArrayID: $ARRAYID \n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
echo "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
command="mpirun -ppn $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np castep.mpi"
pp_path="../../CASTEP_pspots/"
output_path="../../database-dft/"
traj_files=(*.lammpstrj) # get all lammpstrj files in the database
traj_file=${traj_files[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID-1]}
output_file=${output_path}/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_dft_1.xyz # remove .xyz from the file name
1. alpha: 10 frames of equilibration, skip every other 2 frames (33)
2. --index="10:90:2" (40)
3. using pp_path, dir_path and task as default
python /home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py \
--command="${command}" \
--dirpath="../../CASTEP_calcs/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_1/calc/" \
--pppath="${pp_path}" \
--filename="${traj_file}" \
--output="${output_file}" \
--task="singlepoint" \
--xcfunc="PBEsol" \
--cutoffenergy=750 \
--kp=0.028 \
--electol=1e-8 \
--index="11:77:2"
They were running without errors until yesterday, when I had the error:
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py", line 6, in <module>
from dft_calc import *
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/dft_calc.py", line 5, in <module>
from ase import units, Atoms
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ase'
For the job with ID 5113464, which was an array job queued for 10 days. I have resent the job using: `sbatch —array=1 —time=00:01:00 -N1 -n1 sub_ase_castep_alpha_1` (script in the path: /home/jiq21/rds/hpc-work/year_3/AgI_mace_train_try2/md-database/1kbar/ , charged to account MOYA-SL3-CPU) and it ran without an environment problem (although obviously timed out before computing anything).
My worry is that I have multiple jobs with a similar script in the queue since the 12th and I am not sure whether they will run successfully or have a problem activating the conda environment. Would you be able to give any suggestions on why the previous job failed? I did not send it while the environment was activated in the login node. I had tried that previously and didn’t succeed on activating the environment.
Kind regards,
Javiera Quiroz
PhD student
Department of Material Science
University of Cambridge

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Charlie,
You don’t need the conda init command, you only to run it once and you’ve already done that. Replace the commands: 
```
source /home/crhw3/.bashrc
conda activate sdc3a_environment_20231219

mpirun -np 76 /home/crhw3/rds/hpc-work/EoR_Work/Cambridge-SKA-SDC3-Foregrounds/pipeline/python ns_gpr_02.py

```
and resubmit your job. The first command sources your bash profile with all the conda init commands which will allow you to run the conda activate command.
Kind regards,
Avraam

The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user is experiencing significant delays with Python environments on CSD3, specifically with micromamba, conda, and the pre-installed miniconda3. Similar issues have been reported in the past, indicating potential ongoing or recurring problems with the filesystem or network I/O [1][2][3][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Acknowledge the issue and inform the user that similar problems have been reported and are being investigated [1][2][3][5].
2. Escalate the issue to the relevant technical team for further investigation.
3. Monitor the situation and update the user on any progress or resolution.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact commands and error messages you are encountering?
- Have you tried running these commands on different nodes or at different times of the day?
- Are there any specific patterns or times when the issue is more pronounced?

HPC Support

- Maintain clear communication with the user regarding the status of the investigation and any steps being taken to resolve the issue.

metrics.user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.

Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:

2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Performance / Python Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing slow Python module imports on the gfx nodes, which seems to be related to the Lustre filesystem where the virtual environment is stored. This issue has been reported by other users and is likely due to ongoing issues with the filesystem [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Acknowledge the issue and inform the user that this is a known problem related to the Lustre filesystem.
2. Advise the user to try using Anaconda for managing Python environments, as this has been reported to resolve similar issues [3].
3. Escalate the ticket to the system administrators for further investigation if the issue persists.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried using Anaconda for managing your Python environment?
- Can you provide the exact commands and error messages you are encountering?
- Have you noticed any patterns or specific times when the issue occurs more frequently?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

Thank you for reporting this issue. We are aware of ongoing problems with the Lustre filesystem that can cause slow Python module imports, especially when using virtual environments stored in /hpc-work.

To mitigate this issue, we recommend trying Anaconda for managing your Python environments, as this has been reported to resolve similar problems [3].

If the issue persists, please provide the exact commands and error messages you are encountering, and let us know if there are any specific patterns or times when the problem occurs more frequently.

We will continue to investigate this issue and keep you updated on any progress.

Best regards,
HPC Support

metrics.user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Extremely slow python library loading

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support team,
I am experiencing an extremely slow loading process of python libraries such as matplotlib.
I am connected to hpc via vscode and I am running a very simple and light task on the login node but the time it takes to load simple libraries is just too much!
Could you please let me know if there are something happening on the server side or if I could do something to speed up things?
Many thanks,
Cheers,
Jimmy

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Extremely Slow Executions

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Sir or Madam,
I am still experiencing extremely slow runtime, when loading python libraries, to the extend, that it is impossible to work. I have been talking to several people, and the problem, which has persisted since yesterday, is also present for them.
I am logged in under lv380
For reproducibility on cpu-q-478 (but also present on gpu and login nodes)
>source /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/.uv_env/bin/activate
>python -v /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/mace_md/cli/entrypoint.py
Already running
>source /home/lv380/rds/hpc-work/thesis/MD/mace-md/.uv_env/bin/activate
>python
>import numpy
Does sometimes take several minutes.
I have been running the exact same commands around 2-3am today. Back then it worked better, which is why I assume it to be related to overall load.
I hope it is possible to resolve this.
Kind regards,
Leonhard Vulpius

[TICKET_SUMMARY] slow import for pytorch

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
I am running a Python script on Wilkes3 that imports PyTorch. The import takes very long, around 5 minutes. I have tested import on my machine from a local installation, and it is much quicker, so I think this is a problem with a filesystem.
Can you advise me on which partition I should install PyTorch to make it import quickly? Currently it is in a virtual environment in ~/rds/hpc-work/.
Thank you for your help,
Alexander

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Nodes very slow

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Sir or Madam,
All nodes (especially login and gpu nodes) seem to be very slow, in particular when running python, e.g. just importing numpy. 
I am using the python loaded by a module command.
This issue has started since today suddenly.
Best regards
Namu Kroupa

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Extremely slow imports

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Good evening,
I’m trying to get back to using the HPC for a big research project (final one thankfully!) and I noticed that imports are very slow. My code is running from /home/ac2123/rds/rds-wjb31-nmt2020/ac2123/pytod-dev. Is there any way we can check why this happens? This does not occur on my engineering server, etc. The installation is straightforward, it requires python 3.10 and pipenv ([https://pipenv.pypa.io/en/latest/](https://pipenv.pypa.io/en/latest/)) and should magically happen by running “pipenv install”.
Alex

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing access issues with RCS storage via FileZilla. The issue is likely related to the TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) configuration, as seen in similar cases [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's TOTP configuration for RCS storage.
2. Ensure the user is using the correct TOTP entry in their authenticator app (CSD3 (SSH Login)) [4].
3. Check if the user has the correct date and time settings on their phone and computer [4].
4. If the issue persists, reset the user's MFA and guide them through the setup process again [1][2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you configured the TOTP for RCS storage using the correct QR code?
- Are the date and time settings on your phone and computer synchronized correctly?
- Have you tried resetting your MFA and setting it up again?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Christina,

It appears that the issue might be related to the TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) configuration for RCS storage. Please ensure that you are using the correct TOTP entry in your authenticator app (CSD3 (SSH Login)). Also, make sure that the date and time settings on your phone and computer are synchronized correctly.

If the issue persists, we may need to reset your MFA and guide you through the setup process again. Please let us know if you have already tried these steps.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always ensure that the user's TOTP configuration is correct before proceeding with other troubleshooting steps.
- Do not share sensitive information such as passwords or TOTP codes with the user.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-72734
[2] : HPCSSUP-51042
[3] : HPCSSUP-57950
[4] : HPCSSUP-60259
[5] : HPCSSUP-54479

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

benchmark_annotation.summary: Unable to log back into R Studio during interactive Session

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Interactive Sessions / RStudio [1][2][3][4][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2][3][4][5]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1][2][3][4][5]
Urgency: Medium [1][2][3][4][5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with RStudio sessions logging out and being unable to reconnect. This issue has been reported in similar cases where the session remains active but the user is unable to reconnect due to a login error [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Direct the user to the Interactive Sessions page and ask them to click "Connect to RStudio Server" [1][2][3][4][5].
2. If the issue persists, ask the user to provide the exact error message and any screenshots [1][2][3][4][5].
3. Check if there are any ongoing issues with the RStudio server or the HPC system [1][2][3][4][5].
4. If the issue is not resolved, escalate to a higher-level support team for further investigation [1][2][3][4][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message you are seeing?
- Have you tried connecting to RStudio from a different browser or device?
- Have you experienced this issue with other interactive sessions or only with RStudio?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your message. It appears you are experiencing issues with RStudio sessions logging out and being unable to reconnect. This issue has been reported in similar cases where the session remains active but the user is unable to reconnect due to a login error.

Please try the following steps:
1. Go to the Interactive Sessions page: [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/pun/sys/dashboard/batch_connect/sessions](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/pun/sys/dashboard/batch_connect/sessions)
2. Click on "Connect to RStudio Server."

If the issue persists, please provide the exact error message and any screenshots. We will also check if there are any ongoing issues with the RStudio server or the HPC system.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not provide login credentials or sensitive information in the ticket [1][2][3][4][5].
- Ensure that the user is following the correct steps to connect to RStudio [1][2][3][4][5].
- Escalate the issue if it cannot be resolved through standard troubleshooting steps [1][2][3][4][5].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54003
[2] : HPCSSUP-74454
[3] : HPCSSUP-74123
[4] : HPCSSUP-88569
[5] : HPCSSUP-99241
metrics.user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!

benchmark_annotation.summary: Problems connecting to RFS network - token password required.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RFS Access
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues accessing the RFS service, which was previously working. The problem appears to be related to authentication, specifically with the use of tokens. This is a common issue and can often be resolved by ensuring the correct credentials are used [1][2][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's network connection to ensure they are correctly connected to CUDN or have a working VPN [1].
2. Confirm the correct connection string for the RFS service [3].
3. Instruct the user to use their Raven credentials (username: BLUE\<CRSid> and Raven password) instead of the token-generated username [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message you are receiving when trying to access the RFS service?
- Have you tried using your Raven credentials (username: BLUE\<CRSid> and Raven password) instead of the token-generated username?
- Can you confirm that your network connection is stable and that you are connected to CUDN or VPN?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patricia,

Thank you for reaching out. It appears that the issue might be related to the credentials you are using to access the RFS service. Please ensure you are using your Raven credentials (username: BLUE\<CRSid> and Raven password) instead of the token-generated username.

Additionally, please verify that your network connection is stable and that you are connected to CUDN or VPN. If the issue persists, please provide the exact error message you are receiving, and we will assist you further.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not provide sensitive information such as passwords or tokens in the ticket.
- Ensure that the user is aware of the correct credentials to use for accessing the RFS service.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-38159
[2] : HPCSSUP-48558
[3] : HPCSSUP-54370
[5] : HPCSSUP-78386

Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Trouble accessing RFS

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Good morning, 
  
My name is Victor, and I’m currently a summer student in PDN’s Library lab (anatomy building, CRSid vgp23). I’ve been using the eplab RFS (directory rfs-ekp25-eplab) for the past few weeks without issue, however yesterday I suddenly stopped being able to access any of it (and currently have no internet access via the same ethernet port I’ve been using until now, port number 105). I’ve tried to forget the network drive connection and re-connect the way I first did, but I keep getting an incorrect user name or password error when trying to sign in with any of my UIS tokens (despite them working fine to sign into eduroam). I’ve tried token reset with no success, and am not sure what the next steps should be. Is there anything else I could try? Please let me know if you need any additional information, I would be happy to supply it. Thank you in advance! 
  
Kind regards, 
  
Victor

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Accessing RFS

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,  
Myself and another colleague in my lab (lh709) are having difficulty accessing the lab's RFS storage and were wondering if you could help us out. 
 
If I connect to smb://rfs.uis.private.cam.ac.uk/ I see rfs-sdt36-turner-lab, 
but neither mine nor lh709's UIS passwords work (following instructions including BLUE).  
We both got the "you've been made a data user" email, though I wasn't about to accept the terms and conditions as the link 404d, but lh709 did accept the terms.  
Many thanks and best wishes,  
Jamie

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Trouble accessing RFS

[CONTEXT]
@Leon Bond I’m not familiar with connecting to RFS, however, is there any possibility that there would be a password error here? Or is there something else amiss?

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=OTHER>
That’s their network token username they are trying to use (the `+g14_1` suffix gives it away), not their Raven one. Laptop probably saved the username from connecting to the VPN. They need to hit ‘more choices’ and enter their username as `ot284` or `BLUE\ot284` and the Raven password.

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hello,
Just following up on this!
Best,
Oscar
[FILE ATTACHMENT]

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=OTHER>
Hello Oscar,
In the screenshot, you are using the username generated for one of your network tokens (https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/network-services/tokens ) - probably saved when you were setting up the VPN connection.
Could you select the ‘More Choices’ option at the bottom and set the username to be ot284 or BLUE\ot284 and use your University Account (Raven) password. That should allow you to connect.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
@Leon Bond I’m not familiar with connecting to RFS, however, is there any possibility that there would be a password error here? Or is there something else amiss?
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=OTHER>
That’s their network token username they are trying to use (the `+g14_1` suffix gives it away), not their Raven one. Laptop probably saved the username from connecting to the VPN. They need to hit ‘more choices’ and enter their username as `ot284` or `BLUE\ot284` and the Raven password.
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hello,
Just following up on this!
Best,
Oscar
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=OTHER>
Hello Oscar,
In the screenshot, you are using the username generated for one of your network tokens (https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/network-services/tokens ) - probably saved when you were setting up the VPN connection.
Could you select the ‘More Choices’ option at the bottom and set the username to be ot284 or BLUE\ot284 and use your University Account (Raven) password. That should allow you to connect.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Thank you, Leon. Using BLUE\ot284 worked, thank you so much for your help!
Best,
Oscar
</MESSAGE>
